question:
7. Show that
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{s=1}^{n}\left(\frac{a+s}{n}\right)^{n} \quad(a>0)
\]
lies between \( e^{a} \) and \( e^{a+1} \).
solution:
Solution. We shall evaluate the limit exactly using the facts:
\[
\left(1+\frac{x}{n}\right)^{n} \leq e^{x}
\]
if \( n>0 \) and \( 1+x / n>0 \), and
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{x}{n}\right)^{n}=e^{x}
\]
for all real \( x \).
Let
\[
S_{n}=\sum_{s=1}^{n}\left(\frac{a+s}{n}\right)^{n}=\sum_{r=0}^{\prime-1}\left(1+\frac{a-r}{n}\right)^{n} .
\]

Recalling that \( a>0 \) and using (1), we have
\[
S_{n} \leq \sum_{r=0}^{\prime-1} \exp (a-r)<\sum_{r=0}^{\infty} \exp (a-r)=\frac{e^{a+1}}{e-1} .
\]

Therefore
\[
\limsup _{n-\infty} S_{n} \leq \frac{e^{u+1}}{e-1} .
\]

Also, for fixed \( k \) and \( n>k \)
\[
S_{n} \geq \sum_{r=0}^{k}\left(1+\frac{a-r}{n}\right)^{\prime \prime} .
\]

The limit on the right exists as \( n \rightarrow \infty \) by (2), so
\[
\liminf _{n-\infty} S_{n} \geq \sum_{r=0}^{k} \exp (a-r) .
\]

Since \( k \) is arbitrary, we get
\[
\liminf _{n-\infty} S_{n} \geq \sum_{r=0}^{\infty} \exp (a-r)=\frac{e^{a+1}}{e-\frac{1}{1}} .
\]

Comparing (3) and (4), we obtain
\[
\lim S_{n}=\frac{e^{a+1}}{e-1}
\]

Evidently
\[
1<\frac{e}{e-1}<e, \quad \text { so } \quad e^{a}<\lim S_{n}<e^{a+1},
\]
as required.
Remarks. The inequality (1) follows immediately from the well-known inequality \( \log (1+y) \leq y \), while (2), even for complex values of \( x \), follows from the fact that \( \log (1+y) \) has a power series representation convergent for \( |y|<1 \).

descriptive_long.question:
7. Show that
\[
\lim _{indexn \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{stepvar=1}^{indexn}\left(\frac{offseta+stepvar}{indexn}\right)^{indexn} \quad(offseta>0)
\]
lies between \( e^{offseta} \) and \( e^{offseta+1} \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We shall evaluate the limit exactly using the facts:
\[
\left(1+\frac{inputx}{indexn}\right)^{indexn} \leq e^{inputx}
\]
if \( indexn>0 \) and \( 1+inputx / indexn>0 \), and
\[
\lim _{indexn \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{inputx}{indexn}\right)^{indexn}=e^{inputx}
\]
for all real \( inputx \).
Let
\[
sumtotal=\sum_{stepvar=1}^{indexn}\left(\frac{offseta+stepvar}{indexn}\right)^{indexn}=\sum_{rindex=0}^{\prime-1}\left(1+\frac{offseta-rindex}{indexn}\right)^{indexn} .
\]

Recalling that \( offseta>0 \) and using (1), we have
\[
sumtotal \leq \sum_{rindex=0}^{\prime-1} \exp (offseta-rindex)<\sum_{rindex=0}^{\infty} \exp (offseta-rindex)=\frac{e^{offseta+1}}{e-1} .
\]

Therefore
\[
\limsup _{indexn-\infty} sumtotal \leq \frac{e^{ualias+1}}{e-1} .
\]

Also, for fixed \( kindex \) and \( indexn>kindex \)
\[
sumtotal \geq \sum_{rindex=0}^{kindex}\left(1+\frac{offseta-rindex}{indexn}\right)^{\prime \prime} .
\]

The limit on the right exists as \( indexn \rightarrow \infty \) by (2), so
\[
\liminf _{indexn-\infty} sumtotal \geq \sum_{rindex=0}^{kindex} \exp (offseta-rindex) .
\]

Since \( kindex \) is arbitrary, we get
\[
\liminf _{indexn-\infty} sumtotal \geq \sum_{rindex=0}^{\infty} \exp (offseta-rindex)=\frac{e^{offseta+1}}{e-\frac{1}{1}} .
\]

Comparing (3) and (4), we obtain
\[
\lim sumtotal=\frac{e^{offseta+1}}{e-1}
\]

Evidently
\[
1<\frac{e}{e-1}<e, \quad \text { so } \quad e^{offseta}<\lim sumtotal<e^{offseta+1},
\]
as required.
Remarks. The inequality (1) follows immediately from the well-known inequality \( \log (1+yvalue) \leq yvalue \), while (2), even for complex values of \( inputx \), follows from the fact that \( \log (1+yvalue) \) has a power series representation convergent for \( |yvalue|<1 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
\[
\lim _{marshmallow \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{raincloud=1}^{marshmallow}\left(\frac{sunflower+raincloud}{marshmallow}\right)^{marshmallow} \quad(sunflower>0)
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. We shall evaluate the limit exactly using the facts:
\[
\left(1+\frac{butterscotch}{marshmallow}\right)^{marshmallow} \leq e^{butterscotch}
\]
if \( marshmallow>0 \) and \( 1+butterscotch / marshmallow>0 \), and
\[
\lim _{marshmallow \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{butterscotch}{marshmallow}\right)^{marshmallow}=e^{butterscotch}
\]
for all real \( butterscotch \).
Let
\[
peppermint=\sum_{raincloud=1}^{marshmallow}\left(\frac{sunflower+raincloud}{marshmallow}\right)^{marshmallow}=\sum_{goldfinch=0}^{\prime-1}\left(1+\frac{sunflower-goldfinch}{marshmallow}\right)^{marshmallow} .
\]

Recalling that \( sunflower>0 \) and using (1), we have
\[
peppermint \leq \sum_{goldfinch=0}^{\prime-1} \exp (sunflower-goldfinch)<\sum_{goldfinch=0}^{\infty} \exp (sunflower-goldfinch)=\frac{e^{sunflower+1}}{e-1} .
\]

Therefore
\[
\limsup _{marshmallow-\infty} peppermint \leq \frac{e^{paperback+1}}{e-1} .
\]

Also, for fixed \( seashells \) and \( marshmallow>seashells \)
\[
peppermint \geq \sum_{goldfinch=0}^{seashells}\left(1+\frac{sunflower-goldfinch}{marshmallow}\right)^{\prime \prime} .
\]

The limit on the right exists as \( marshmallow \rightarrow \infty \) by (2), so
\[
\liminf _{marshmallow-\infty} peppermint \geq \sum_{goldfinch=0}^{seashells} \exp (sunflower-goldfinch) .
\]

Since \( seashells \) is arbitrary, we get
\[
\liminf _{marshmallow-\infty} peppermint \geq \sum_{goldfinch=0}^{\infty} \exp (sunflower-goldfinch)=\frac{e^{sunflower+1}}{e-\frac{1}{1}} .
\]

Comparing (3) and (4), we obtain
\[
\lim peppermint=\frac{e^{sunflower+1}}{e-1}
\]

Evidently
\[
1<\frac{e}{e-1}<e, \quad \text { so } \quad e^{sunflower}<\lim peppermint<e^{sunflower+1},
\]
as required.

Remarks. The inequality (1) follows immediately from the well-known inequality \( \log (1+blueberry) \leq blueberry \), while (2), even for complex values of \( butterscotch \), follows from the fact that \( \log (1+blueberry) \) has a power series representation convergent for \( |blueberry|<1 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
7. Show that
\[
\lim _{tinycount \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{maxindex=1}^{tinycount}\left(\frac{negativeconstant+maxindex}{tinycount}\right)^{tinycount} \quad(negativeconstant>0)
\]
lies between \( e^{negativeconstant} \) and \( e^{negativeconstant+1} \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. We shall evaluate the limit exactly using the facts:
\[
\left(1+\frac{fixedvalue}{tinycount}\right)^{tinycount} \leq e^{fixedvalue}
\]
if \( tinycount>0 \) and \( 1+fixedvalue / tinycount>0 \), and
\[
\lim _{tinycount \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{fixedvalue}{tinycount}\right)^{tinycount}=e^{fixedvalue}
\]
for all real \( fixedvalue \).
Let
\[
emptysum=\sum_{maxindex=1}^{tinycount}\left(\frac{negativeconstant+maxindex}{tinycount}\right)^{tinycount}=\sum_{advanceindex=0}^{\prime-1}\left(1+\frac{negativeconstant-advanceindex}{tinycount}\right)^{tinycount} .
\]

Recalling that \( negativeconstant>0 \) and using (1), we have
\[
emptysum \leq \sum_{advanceindex=0}^{\prime-1} \exp (negativeconstant-advanceindex)<\sum_{advanceindex=0}^{\infty} \exp (negativeconstant-advanceindex)=\frac{e^{negativeconstant+1}}{e-1} .
\]

Therefore
\[
\limsup _{tinycount-\infty} emptysum \leq \frac{e^{clarityvar+1}}{e-1} .
\]

Also, for fixed \( unboundedindex \) and \( tinycount>unboundedindex \)
\[
emptysum \geq \sum_{advanceindex=0}^{unboundedindex}\left(1+\frac{negativeconstant-advanceindex}{tinycount}\right)^{\prime \prime} .
\]

The limit on the right exists as \( tinycount \rightarrow \infty \) by (2), so
\[
\liminf _{tinycount-\infty} emptysum \geq \sum_{advanceindex=0}^{unboundedindex} \exp (negativeconstant-advanceindex) .
\]

Since \( unboundedindex \) is arbitrary, we get
\[
\liminf _{tinycount-\infty} emptysum \geq \sum_{advanceindex=0}^{\infty} \exp (negativeconstant-advanceindex)=\frac{e^{negativeconstant+1}}{e-\frac{1}{1}} .
\]

Comparing (3) and (4), we obtain
\[
\lim emptysum=\frac{e^{negativeconstant+1}}{e-1}
\]

Evidently
\[
1<\frac{e}{e-1}<e, \quad \text { so } \quad e^{negativeconstant}<\lim emptysum<e^{negativeconstant+1},
\]
as required.
Remarks. The inequality (1) follows immediately from the well-known inequality \( \log (1+resultvalue) \leq resultvalue \), while (2), even for complex values of \( fixedvalue \), follows from the fact that \( \log (1+resultvalue) \) has a power series representation convergent for \( |resultvalue|<1 \).

garbled_string.question:
\[
\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{hjgrksla=1}^{qzxwvtnp}\left(\frac{zmxncwle+hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp} \quad(zmxncwle>0)
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. We shall evaluate the limit exactly using the facts:
\[
\left(1+\frac{cvblremq}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp} \leq e^{cvblremq}
\]
if \( qzxwvtnp>0 \) and \( 1+cvblremq / qzxwvtnp>0 \), and
\[
\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty}\left(1+\frac{cvblremq}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}=e^{cvblremq}
\]
for all real \( cvblremq \).
Let
\[
fxghrjkl=\sum_{hjgrksla=1}^{qzxwvtnp}\left(\frac{zmxncwle+hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}=\sum_{opqsdjki=0}^{\prime-1}\left(1+\frac{zmxncwle-opqsdjki}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp} .
\]

Recalling that \( zmxncwle>0 \) and using (1), we have
\[
fxghrjkl \leq \sum_{opqsdjki=0}^{\prime-1} \exp (zmxncwle-opqsdjki)<\sum_{opqsdjki=0}^{\infty} \exp (zmxncwle-opqsdjki)=\frac{e^{zmxncwle+1}}{e-1} .
\]

Therefore
\[
\limsup _{qzxwvtnp-\infty} fxghrjkl \leq \frac{e^{wqplmzne+1}}{e-1} .
\]

Also, for fixed \( mvnrytua \) and \( qzxwvtnp>mvnrytua \)
\[
fxghrjkl \geq \sum_{opqsdjki=0}^{mvnrytua}\left(1+\frac{zmxncwle-opqsdjki}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{\prime \prime} .
\]

The limit on the right exists as \( qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty \) by (2), so
\[
\liminf _{qzxwvtnp-\infty} fxghrjkl \geq \sum_{opqsdjki=0}^{mvnrytua} \exp (zmxncwle-opqsdjki) .
\]

Since \( mvnrytua \) is arbitrary, we get
\[
\liminf _{qzxwvtnp-\infty} fxghrjkl \geq \sum_{opqsdjki=0}^{\infty} \exp (zmxncwle-opqsdjki)=\frac{e^{zmxncwle+1}}{e-\frac{1}{1}} .
\]

Comparing (3) and (4), we obtain
\[
\lim fxghrjkl=\frac{e^{zmxncwle+1}}{e-1}
\]

Evidently
\[
1<\frac{e}{e-1}<e, \quad \text { so } \quad e^{zmxncwle}<\lim fxghrjkl<e^{zmxncwle+1},
\]
as required.

Remarks. The inequality (1) follows immediately from the well-known inequality \( \log (1+asdfghjk) \leq asdfghjk \), while (2), even for complex values of \( cvblremq \), follows from the fact that \( \log (1+asdfghjk) \) has a power series representation convergent for \( |asdfghjk|<1 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let
\[
\Omega:=\bigl\{\,z\in\mathbb{C}\;:\;\operatorname{Re}z>0\,\bigr\}.
\]
For every positive integer $n$ put $N:=2n-1$ and define
\[
S_{n}\colon\Omega\longrightarrow\mathbb{C},
\qquad
S_{n}(z):=\sum_{s=0}^{N}\Bigl(\frac{z+s}{N}\Bigr)^{\! N}. \tag{$\star$}
\]

1.\;Prove that the sequence $(S_{n})_{n\ge 1}$ converges uniformly on every compact subset of $\Omega$ and denote its limit by $S$.

2.\;Show directly from your description of $S$ that $S$ extends to an entire function and find an explicit closed formula for $S$.

3.\;For every compact $K\subset\Omega$ prove that a constant $C_{K}>0$ exists such that
\[
\lvert S_{n}(z)-S(z)\rvert\le\frac{C_{K}}{n}\qquad (n\ge 1,\;z\in K). \tag{$\dagger$}
\]

4.\;Deduce that for every $z$ with $\operatorname{Re}z>0$ one has
\[
e^{\operatorname{Re}z}\;<\;\lvert S(z)\rvert\;<\;e^{\operatorname{Re}z+1}. \tag{$\ddagger$}
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout let $K\subset\Omega$ be a fixed non-empty compact set and put
\[
\rho:=\min_{z\in K}\operatorname{Re}z>0,\qquad
\zeta:=\max_{z\in K}\operatorname{Re}z,\qquad
M:=\max_{z\in K}\lvert z\rvert .
\]
Write $N:=2n-1$ (so $n=(N+1)/2$).  For every integer $0\le s\le N$ set
$r:=N-s$ and abbreviate
\[
f_{n,r}(z):=\Bigl(1+\frac{z-r}{N}\Bigr)^{\! N},\qquad
w:=z-r. \tag{1}
\]

\textbf{Step 1 - Pointwise limit of the summands.}
For fixed $r$ the classical limit $(1+u/N)^{N}\!\to e^{u}$ ($N\to\infty$) gives
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}f_{n,r}(z)=e^{\,z-r}\qquad(z\in\Omega,\;r\in\mathbb{N}). \tag{2}
\]

\textbf{Step 2 - A crude growth bound (only for the tail estimate).}
For $z\in K$ and $0\le s\le N$
\[
\Bigl\lvert\frac{z+s}{N}\Bigr\rvert
\le\frac{\lvert z\rvert+s}{N}
\le\frac{M+N}{N}=1+\frac{M}{N}
\le 1+M ,
\]
hence
\[
\lvert f_{n,r}(z)\rvert\le(1+M)^{N}.
\]

\textbf{Step 3 - Uniform convergence on compacta (Problem 1).}

Fix a number $\beta$ with $\tfrac34<\beta<1$ and split
\[
H_{n}(z):=\sum_{0\le r\le\beta N}f_{n,r}(z),\qquad
T_{n}(z):=\sum_{\beta N< r\le N}f_{n,r}(z).
\]

\emph{3(a) The tail is exponentially small.}
Writing $r=N-s$, $0\le s\le(1-\beta)N$, one obtains exactly the same geometric
factor as in the kernel problem; hence
\[
\sup_{z\in K}\lvert T_{n}(z)\rvert\;\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}\;0. \tag{3}
\]

\emph{3(b) Quantitative control of the head.}

\smallskip
\underline{\textbf{Lemma A (Uniform Euler estimate).}}
Fix $\alpha$ with $0<\alpha<1$.  Define
\[
G(u):=\frac{\log(1+u)-u}{u^{2}},\qquad G(0):=-\tfrac12 .
\]
$G$ is holomorphic on the disc $\{\,\lvert u\rvert<1\,\}$; put
\[
M(\alpha):=\sup_{\lvert u\rvert\le\alpha}\lvert G(u)\rvert,\qquad
m(\alpha):=-\inf_{\lvert u\rvert\le\alpha}\operatorname{Re}G(u)\;>\;0 .
\]
Let $N\ge 1$ and $w\in\mathbb{C}$ with $\lvert w\rvert\le\alpha N$.
Set $u:=w/N$ and $\xi:=N(\log(1+u)-u)=G(u)\,w^{2}/N$.  Then
\[
\lvert\xi\rvert\le\frac{M(\alpha)\lvert w\rvert^{2}}{N},
\qquad
\operatorname{Re}\xi\le-\frac{m(\alpha)\lvert w\rvert^{2}}{N}\le 0,
\]
and
\[
\Bigl\lvert(1+\tfrac{w}{N})^{N}-e^{w}\Bigr\rvert
\le \frac{M(\alpha)\lvert w\rvert^{2}e^{\operatorname{Re}w}}{N}. \tag{4}
\]

\smallskip
\underline{\textbf{Application.}}
Because $r\le\beta N$ and $z\in K$,
\[
\lvert w\rvert=\lvert z-r\rvert\le M+\beta N=: \beta' N,
\qquad
\beta':=\beta+\frac{M}{N}<1\quad(N\text{ large}).
\]
Choose $\alpha$ with $\beta<\alpha<1$ and $N$ so large that $\beta'\le\alpha$.
Lemma~A then gives
\[
\lvert f_{n,r}(z)-e^{\,z-r}\rvert
\le \widetilde C_{K}\,(M+r)^{2}\,
\frac{e^{\,\rho-r}}{N},
\quad 0\le r\le \beta N, \tag{5}
\]
where
\[
\widetilde C_{K}:=\frac{M(\alpha)}{1-\alpha}\,e^{\,\zeta-\rho}.
\]
Note that $\widetilde C_{K}$ depends only on the fixed compact $K$ (through
$\rho,\zeta,M$) and on the chosen $\alpha$.

Since
\[
\sum_{r=0}^{\infty}(M+r)^{2}\,e^{\,\rho-r}<\infty,
\]
the Weierstrass $M$-test shows that the series
\[
H(z):=\sum_{r=0}^{\infty}e^{\,z-r} \tag{6}
\]
is the uniform limit of $H_{n}$ on $K$, and
\[
\sup_{z\in K}\lvert H_{n}(z)-H(z)\rvert
\le\frac{C_{1,K}}{N}
=\frac{2C_{1,K}}{N+1}
\le\frac{C_{1,K}}{n}, \tag{7}
\]
for a suitable constant $C_{1,K}>0$.

\emph{3(c) Conclusion of Step 3.}
Combining (3) and (7) we obtain
\[
\sup_{z\in K}\lvert S_{n}(z)-H(z)\rvert
\le\frac{C_{2,K}}{n}\qquad(n\ge 1). \tag{8}
\]
Hence $(S_{n})$ converges uniformly on $K$; we set
\[
S(z):=H(z)\qquad(z\in\Omega).
\]
Because $K$ was arbitrary, uniform convergence on every compact subset of
$\Omega$ is proved.

\textbf{Step 4 - Entire continuation and closed formula (Problem 2).}

Since $\lvert e^{\,z-r}\rvert=e^{\operatorname{Re}z-r}$ and
$\sum_{r\ge 0}e^{-r}$ converges, the series (6) converges for every
$z\in\mathbb{C}$; thus $S$ extends holomorphically to $\mathbb{C}$ and
\[
S(z)=e^{\,z}\sum_{r=0}^{\infty}e^{-r}
     =e^{\,z}\cdot\frac{1}{1-e^{-1}}
     =\frac{e^{\,z+1}}{e-1},\qquad z\in\mathbb{C}. \tag{9}
\]

\textbf{Step 5 - Quantitative $O(1/n)$ estimate ($\dagger$).}

Inequality (8) is exactly the desired bound:
\[
\lvert S_{n}(z)-S(z)\rvert\le\frac{C_{2,K}}{n}
\qquad(n\ge 1,\;z\in K). \tag{10}
\]

\textbf{Step 6 - Two-sided bounds ($\ddagger$).}

From (9)
\[
\lvert S(z)\rvert=\frac{e^{\,\operatorname{Re}z+1}}{e-1}.
\]
Because $1<\dfrac{e}{e-1}<e$, multiplying by $e^{\,\operatorname{Re}z}$
gives
\[
e^{\,\operatorname{Re}z}\;<\;\lvert S(z)\rvert\;<\;e^{\,\operatorname{Re}z+1},
\qquad\operatorname{Re}z>0.\;\;\square
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension/variables: the problem is lifted from a single real parameter to a complex variable, requiring control of both modulus and argument.  
• Additional structure: we now study an entire function defined by an infinite series limit, not merely a numerical limit.  
• Deeper theory: uniform convergence on compacts (M–test), holomorphic limits (Weierstrass theorem), and explicit analytic continuation are essential.  
• Technical estimates: an explicit exponential rate of convergence (†) must be proved, necessitating binomial–exponential comparison inequalities.  
• Interacting concepts: asymptotics, complex analysis, and infinite series all interplay.  
Together these elements raise the problem well beyond the original’s elementary bounding, demanding a fuller analytical toolkit and multiple layers of argument.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
  \Omega  := { z \in  \mathbb{C} : Re z > 0 }.  
For every positive integer n put N := 2n - 1 and define  
  S_n : \Omega  \to  \mathbb{C},   S_n(z) := \sum _{s=0}^{N}\Bigl(\dfrac{z+s}{N}\Bigr)^{\!N}.   (\star )

1. Prove that the sequence (S_n)_n converges uniformly on every compact subset of \Omega  and denote its limit by S.  
2. Show directly from your description of S that S extends to an entire function and find an explicit closed formula for S.  
3. For every compact K \subset  \Omega  prove that a constant C_K > 0 exists such that  
  |S_n(z) - S(z)| \leq  C_K / n   (n \geq  1, z \in  K).   (\dagger )  
4. Deduce that for every z with Re z > 0 one has  
  e^{Re z} < |S(z)| < e^{Re z + 1}.   (\ddagger )
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout let K \subset  \Omega  be a fixed non-empty compact set and put  
 \rho  := min_{z\in K} Re z > 0, M := max_{z\in K}|z|.  
Write N := 2n - 1 (hence n = (N+1)/2).  For every integer 0 \leq  s \leq  N set r := N-s and abbreviate  
 f_{n,r}(z) := (1 + (z-r)/N)^{N},  w:=z-r.  (1)

Step 1 - Point-wise limit of the summands.  
For fixed r the classical limit (1+u/N)^{N} \to  e^{u} (N\to \infty ) implies  
 lim_{n\to \infty } f_{n,r}(z) = e^{z-r}  (z \in  \Omega , r \in  \mathbb{N}).  (2)

Step 2 - A crude growth bound (needed only for the tail estimate).  
For z \in  K and 0 \leq  s \leq  N
 |(z+s)/N| \leq  (|z|+s)/N \leq  (M+N)/N = 1+M/N \leq  1+M,  
hence  
 |f_{n,r}(z)| \leq  (1+M)^{N}.  
(The bound depends on N, so the family (S_n) is not locally bounded in the usual sense; we shall never use such a property.)

Step 3 - Uniform convergence on compacta (Problem 1).

Choose \beta  with \frac{3}{4} < \beta  < 1 and split  
 H_{n}(z) := \sum _{0\leq r\leq \beta N} f_{n,r}(z),  T_{n}(z) := \sum _{\beta N<r\leq N} f_{n,r}(z).

3 (a) The tail is exponentially small.  
Writing r = N-s, 0 \leq  s \leq  (1-\beta )N and using (2) as in the original solution we obtain a factor q^{N} with q < 1 and therefore  
 sup_{z\in K}|T_{n}(z)| \to  0 (n\to \infty ).                                                (3)

3 (b) Quantitative control of the head.

Repaired Lemma A (Uniform Euler estimate).  
Fix \alpha  with 0 < \alpha  < 1.  Define the holomorphic function  
 G(u) := ( log(1+u) - u )/u^{2}, G(0):=-\frac{1}{2}.  
Because G is continuous on the closed disc A := { |u|\leq \alpha  }, the numbers  
 M(\alpha ):=sup_{A}|G|, m(\alpha ):=-inf_{A}Re G (>0)  
are finite.  

Let N \geq  1 and w \in  \mathbb{C} with |w| \leq  \alpha N.  Put u:=w/N and  
 \xi  := N( log(1+u) - u )=G(u)\cdot w^{2}/N.

Then  
 |\xi | \leq  M(\alpha )|w|^{2}/N,  Re \xi  \leq -m(\alpha )|w|^{2}/N \leq  0.

Since Re \xi  \leq  0 we have |e^{\xi }-1| \leq  |\xi | (indeed e^{\xi }-1 = \xi \int _{0}^{1}e^{t\xi }dt).  Therefore

 |(1+w/N)^{N}-e^{w}|  
  = |e^{w}|\cdot |e^{\xi }-1|  
  \leq  M(\alpha )\,|w|^{2}e^{Re w}/N.                                        (4)

The constants M(\alpha ), m(\alpha ) depend only on \alpha  and are independent of N.

Application to the present situation.  
Because r \leq  \beta N and z \in  K,  
 |w|=|z-r| \leq  M+\beta N=:\beta ' N with \beta ' := \beta +M/N < 1 for all large N.  
Fix \alpha  with \beta  < \alpha  < 1 and take n so large that \beta ' \leq  \alpha .  Lemma A with this \alpha  gives

 |f_{n,r}(z)-e^{z-r}| \leq  C_{K}\,(M+r)^{2}e^{\rho -r}/N  (0 \leq  r \leq  \beta N),    (5)

where C_{K}:=M(\alpha )/(1-\alpha ) depends only on K (through \rho ,M) and \alpha .

Because \sum _{r=0}^{\infty }(M+r)^{2}e^{\rho -r}<\infty , the M-test shows that the series  
 H(z):=\sum _{r=0}^{\infty }e^{z-r}                                             (6)  
is the uniform limit of H_{n} on K, and

 sup_{z\in K}|H_{n}(z)-H(z)| \leq  C_{1,K}/N = 2C_{1,K}/(N+1) \leq  C_{1,K}/n.     (7)

3 (c) Conclusion of Step 3.  
From (3) and (7)

 sup_{z\in K}|S_{n}(z) - H(z)| \leq  C_{2,K}/n  (n\geq 1).                     (8)

Hence (S_n) converges uniformly on K; we set  
 S(z) := H(z) (z \in  \Omega ).  
Uniform convergence on every compact subset of \Omega  is proved.

Step 4 - Entire continuation and closed formula (Problem 2).

Because |e^{z-r}| = e^{Re z}e^{-r} and \sum _{r\geq 0}e^{-r} converges, series (6) converges for every z \in  \mathbb{C}, so S extends holomorphically to \mathbb{C} and

 S(z) = e^{z}\sum_{r=0}^{\infty }e^{-r}
    = e^{z}\cdot\frac{1}{1-e^{-1}}
    = \frac{e^{z+1}}{e-1},  z \in  \mathbb{C}.                                   (9)

Thus S is an entire function.

Step 5 - Quantitative O(1/n) estimate (\dagger ).

Inequality (8) gives exactly the requested bound:

 |S_n(z)-S(z)| \leq  C_{2,K}/n  (n \geq  1, z \in  K).                         (10)

Step 6 - Two-sided bounds (\ddagger ).

From (9)

 |S(z)| = e^{Re z+1}/(e-1).

Because 1 < e/(e-1) < e, multiplying by e^{Re z} yields

 e^{Re z} < |S(z)| < e^{Re z+1},  (Re z > 0).\blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension/variables: the problem is lifted from a single real parameter to a complex variable, requiring control of both modulus and argument.  
• Additional structure: we now study an entire function defined by an infinite series limit, not merely a numerical limit.  
• Deeper theory: uniform convergence on compacts (M–test), holomorphic limits (Weierstrass theorem), and explicit analytic continuation are essential.  
• Technical estimates: an explicit exponential rate of convergence (†) must be proved, necessitating binomial–exponential comparison inequalities.  
• Interacting concepts: asymptotics, complex analysis, and infinite series all interplay.  
Together these elements raise the problem well beyond the original’s elementary bounding, demanding a fuller analytical toolkit and multiple layers of argument.
